title: Ankara Summit Looms as Erdoğan Leverages Strategic Weight, Democracy Crackdown Intensifies
subhead: With the July 7-8 NATO summit days away, Erdoğan tightens his grip at home while extracting concessions abroad — Trump approves F-110 engine sales, NATO accreditation denies opposition journalists, and a comedian is detained. Meanwhile, the Kurdish peace framework law nears parliament, and Fidan carries Ankara's message to Moscow.

sections.heading: NATO Ankara Summit: Erdoğan's Stage for Legitimacy
sections.body:
The July 7-8 NATO summit in Ankara will take place at Erdoğan's 1,150-room Bestepe presidential compound, a venue choice that itself signals how far Turkey's standing in the alliance has shifted. A decade ago, European allies blocked Turkey's bid to host the 2018 summit over democratic backsliding. This time, no such objection was raised.

CFR analyst Henri Barkey writes that Erdoğan intends to use the summit to present himself as an 'indispensable ally' to Europeans and a valued friend to Trump — legitimizing not just his foreign policy but his authoritarian domestic rule. The timing is pointed: hundreds have been arrested in a security sweep ahead of the summit, and NATO has denied press accreditation to opposition journalists, making the alliance, in Barkey's words, 'a collaborator in blatantly undemocratic conduct.'

According to the Irish Times, NATO headquarters in Brussels now refers to Turkey as 'an indispensable partner' second only to the US. Finland's President Alexander Stubb put it plainly in June: 'We need to open our minds to the understanding that Turkey needs to be as close to us as possible from a security perspective.' The shift from pariah to partner is nearly complete.
sections.whyItMatters: The summit gives Erdoğan the international validation he has sought for two decades, at precisely the moment he is dismantling what remains of Turkey's democratic institutions. Western silence on rights concerns is not an oversight — it's a transaction.

sections.heading: Trump's Gift Bag: F-110 Engines and F-35 Hints
sections.body:
On the eve of the summit, Trump notified Congress of his intention to sell Turkey roughly 80 F-110 aircraft engines for its domestically produced KAAN fifth-generation fighter, a deal long stalled on Capitol Hill, according to CFR. Vice President JD Vance has also suggested the administration is examining whether Turkey can be readmitted to the F-35 program, from which it was expelled after purchasing the Russian S-400 missile system — despite no change in the S-400's status on Turkish soil.

Trump has praised Erdoğan at every opportunity, telling reporters he would not have attended the summit without Erdoğan's personal solicitation. He has twice withdrawn US troops from the Syria-Turkey border, endorsing Turkish operations that displaced more than 180,000 Kurds. After Hayat Tahrir al-Sham toppled Assad, Trump embraced the new Syrian leader Ahmed al-Sharaa as a 'great young, attractive guy' and lifted Syria sanctions — handing Ankara another regional win.
sections.whyItMatters: The engine sale and F-35 talk represent a direct override of congressional restrictions and US defense priorities in favor of personal diplomacy with Erdoğan. The KAAN program, powered by American engines, would make Turkey the only non-Western nation building a fifth-generation stealth fighter.

sections.heading: US Decoupling from Europe Creates Opening for Turkey
sections.body:
A second CFR analysis, by Liana Fix and Anna Terkhorn, paints a stark picture: the US is actively decoupling from European security. Plans reportedly include reducing F-16 and F-15 jets assigned to NATO by a third, cutting strategic bombers by half, and removing a missile-launching submarine and aircraft carrier from NATO's crisis pool. About 5,000 troops are being withdrawn from Germany over 6-12 months.

Europeans had hoped for a structured transition — a 'NATO 3.0' roadmap. Instead, they face unilateral withdrawals without waiting for European capability gaps to be filled. Key shortfalls include ISR, air and missile defense, long-range fires, and cyber/electronic warfare.

Turkey is positioning itself to fill part of this gap. Irish Times reports that well over half of Turkey's $10 billion in annual defense exports went to NATO countries last year. Companies like Arca Savunma — founded just six years ago by a former NATO procurement official — have signed multibillion-dollar deals with Slovakia, Bulgaria, and Estonia. Bayraktar drones are already deployed across European armies.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey's defense industry is becoming structurally important to European security at the exact moment the US is pulling back. This isn't soft power — it's hard industrial dependency that will shape alliance politics for years.

sections.heading: Democratic Space Shrinks: Comedian Detained, Opposition Silenced
sections.body:
Stand-up comedian Deniz Goktas was detained at Istanbul airport on Thursday upon returning from abroad, after his political comedy special 'Olu Deniz' amassed 8.5 million views on YouTube, according to AP. Prosecutors launched an investigation into 'publicly denigrating religious values,' but the show's sharpest content targeted Erdoğan's 32-year political career, the jailing of Istanbul mayor Ekrem Imamoglu, and the court-ordered ousting of CHP leader Ozgur Ozel. AP notes that dozens of actors, singers, and influencers have been detained in police raids as part of investigations into alleged drug use — a pattern of using non-political charges to suppress cultural figures.

Separately, more than 200 people were detained in a pre-summit security sweep, including over three dozen environmentalists in their sixties and seventies, according to the Irish Times. Reuters reported that NATO allies have grown silent on rights concerns in Turkey, with US and European leaders making no public comment on the court-ordered removal of opposition leader Ozel in May.

As Seren Selvin Korkmaz of IstanPol Institute told the Irish Times: 'Democratic institutions shape the credibility of Turkey's strategic strength. They provide the institutional infrastructure that allows international agreements to last beyond individual leaders.'
sections.whyItMatters: The Goktas detention is a litmus test: a comedian with 8.5 million views is detained on a religious-values pretext the day before a NATO summit in the same city. Western leaders' silence signals that human rights concerns are now fully subordinate to strategic calculations.

sections.heading: Kurdish Peace Framework Law Nears Parliament
sections.body:
Turkey's long-stalled Kurdish peace process is regaining momentum. Parliament Speaker Numan Kurtulmus described the upcoming framework law as 'standalone and temporary' — a one-time measure covering members of the dissolved PKK, with a limited application window: 'The door will be closed for those who do not come.'

According to BBC Turkce, Erdoğan instructed his party members to ensure the law passes before parliament goes on recess. Pro-government newspaper Turkiye reported that implementation will be conditioned on a verification mechanism: MIT (intelligence) and the Turkish Armed Forces will prepare a report on PKK disarmament, which the National Security Council must confirm. Government sources said the satisfactory rate for weapons laying-down and cave evacuations must reach at least 80 percent before the law takes effect.

The pro-Kurdish DEM Party is wary. Co-Chair Tuncer Bakirhan said the law 'cannot be postponed' and 'cannot be left until autumn', but warned it must not be narrow, vague, or unilateral. Deputy Co-Chair Tayip Temel expressed concern the government would present a fait accompli: 'We hope the government will not produce a unilateral text and say take this and sign it.' DEM officials also flagged concerns about excluding PKK leader Abdullah Ocalan from the process and about categorization of individuals.

The process began after MHP leader Devlet Bahceli suggested in October 2024 that Ocalan could be released under the 'right to hope' provision. The PKK declared dissolution in May 2025 and held a symbolic disarmament ceremony. A parliamentary commission completed its work in December 2025, but no concrete legislative steps followed until now.
sections.whyItMatters: The framework law is the first tangible legislative step after a year of stagnation. But the government's conditionality — 80% disarmament verification before implementation — and DEM's insistence on a broad, negotiated text set up a clash over scope that could derail the process before it starts.

sections.heading: Fidan Carries Ankara's Message to Moscow
sections.body:
Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan met with Vladimir Putin in Moscow this week, telling CNN Turk that he was carrying messages between Erdoğan and the Russian president. Fidan said it was important for Turkey to hear Putin's positions firsthand on the Ukraine conflict, the Caucasus peace process, and the situations in Palestine, Gaza, Lebanon, and Syria.

According to TASS, Fidan said Putin adheres to the agreements reached with Trump in Anchorage in August 2025 regarding the Ukraine peace process. Fidan's visit also covered Black Sea shipping safety — in recent months, Ukraine and Russia have accused each other of drone attacks on tankers near Turkey's northern coast — and Turkey's proposal for a limited ceasefire on ports and energy infrastructure.

The timing is deliberate: with the NATO summit days away, Fidan's Moscow trip signals that Turkey can talk to both sides simultaneously. Al-Monitor notes that Ukraine's ambassador to Ankara has sought deeper defense industry ties with Turkey, including joint production — a further sign that Ankara is hedging between Kyiv and Moscow rather than choosing.
sections.whyItMatters: Fidan's Moscow visit is part of a calculated pattern: Turkey mediates between Russia and the West while maintaining trade and tourism ties with Moscow. The Ankara summit will showcase Turkey's NATO credentials; the Moscow trip reminds everyone of its independent lane.

sections.heading: Carney's Defence Bank and the Global Defence Realignment
sections.body:
Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney is pushing a 10-country, $133 billion Defence, Security and Resilience Bank (DSRB) to be announced at the Ankara summit. Led by Canada's chief negotiator Isabelle Hudon, the proposal aims to provide affordable financing for allied defense capabilities — filling part of the gap left by US retrenchment. Devdiscourse reports the bank would offer up to 100 billion pounds in financing, though its viability depends on securing triple-A credit backing from key nations. Productive talks have been held with South Korea.

The initiative reflects a broader shift: if the US is decoupling from European security, middle powers are organizing alternative financing structures. For Turkey, the DSRB could provide capital for its expanding defense industry — or compete with it, depending on how membership is structured.
sections.whyItMatters: The DSRB is a concrete attempt to institutionalize European defense autonomy outside the US umbrella. Whether Turkey is included as a founder or treated as a supplier will shape who profits from the continent's rearmament.

bottomLine: Five days before the NATO summit opens in Ankara, the strategic picture is set: Erdoğan has extracted major concessions from Trump (F-110 engines, F-35 talk, Syria sanctions relief) while intensifying his domestic crackdown (comedian detained, 200+ arrested in security sweep, opposition journalists denied NATO accreditation). Europe, facing an accelerating US decoupling, is embracing Turkey's defense industry and strategic location despite its democratic erosion. The Kurdish peace framework law is finally moving toward parliament — but with conditionality that could stall it. Meanwhile, Fidan's Moscow trip and Carney's defence bank bid show Ankara operating on multiple tracks simultaneously: NATO host, Russian interlocutor, and defense-industrial powerbroker. The summit will ratify all of this on a global stage.
sources: CFR, Irish Times, Bloomberg, Reuters, AP, TASS, Daily Sabah, bianet, WSWS, Devdiscourse, Al-Monitor, The Economist
